report:
## Profitability

Coca-Cola (KO) and PepsiCo (PEP) both show revenue growth over the FY2022–FY2025 period, but their profitability trajectories diverge sharply in the most recent year. KO grew revenues from $43.0B in FY2022 [KO:Q0] to $47.9B in FY2025 [KO:Q3], while PEP operates at a much larger scale, expanding from $86.4B [PEP:Q0] to $93.9B [PEP:Q3]. On gross margin, KO's gross profit rose from $25.0B [KO:Q12] to $29.5B [KO:Q15], implying a gross margin of roughly 61.6% in FY2025—meaningfully higher than PEP's $50.9B gross profit [PEP:Q15] on $93.9B in revenue, or about 54.1%, reflecting PEP's heavier exposure to lower-margin food and beverage manufacturing. The most striking divergence is at the bottom line in FY2025: KO's net income surged to $13.1B [KO:Q7] (a net margin of ~27.3%), while PEP's net income declined to approximately $8.2B [PEP:Q7] (a net margin of roughly 8.8%), down from around $9.6B in FY2024 [PEP:Q6]—though it should be noted that the PEP citation anchors for these figures are not fully enumerated in the available citations block, even as the underlying numbers are confirmed by direct XBRL retrieval. Operating income tells a similar story—KO's operating income jumped to $13.8B in FY2025 [KO:Q11] after a dip to $10.0B in FY2024 [KO:Q10], whereas PEP's operating income declined to $11.5B in FY2025 [PEP:Q11] from a peak of $12.9B in FY2024 [PEP:Q10]. Overall, KO demonstrates superior margin quality and a strong FY2025 earnings rebound, while PEP faces margin compression despite its larger revenue base.

**KO:** Coca-Cola delivered a strong profitability rebound in FY2025, with net income reaching $13.1B [KO:Q7] and operating income of $13.8B [KO:Q11] on revenues of $47.9B [KO:Q3], translating to a net margin of approximately 27.3%—the highest in the four-year window shown. Its gross margin of roughly 61.6% [KO:Q15] reflects the premium pricing power of its concentrate-heavy business model, and the FY2025 results represent a meaningful recovery from the operating income dip seen in FY2024 [KO:Q10].

**PEP:** PepsiCo's profitability came under pressure in FY2025, with net income declining to approximately $8.2B [PEP:Q7] from around $9.6B in FY2024 [PEP:Q6], and operating income falling to $11.5B [PEP:Q11] from a peak of $12.9B [PEP:Q10], even as revenues grew modestly to $93.9B [PEP:Q3]. While PEP's absolute gross profit of $50.9B [PEP:Q15] dwarfs KO's in dollar terms, its gross and net margins trail KO's considerably, reflecting the cost pressures inherent in its diversified snack and beverage portfolio. *(Note: PEP net income citation tags are not fully enumerated in the available citations block; figures are confirmed via direct XBRL retrieval but independent sourcing-chain verification is limited.)*

profitability.notes: Every quantitative claim in the analyst report was verified against retrieved XBRL data. KO revenues ($43.0B FY2022 → $47.9B FY2025), PEP revenues ($86.4B → $93.9B), KO gross profit ($25.0B → $29.5B), PEP gross profit ($50.9B FY2025), derived gross margins (KO ~61.6%, PEP ~54.2%), KO net income ($13.1B, ~27.3% margin), PEP net income ($8.2B, ~8.8% margin, down from $9.578B in FY2024 reported as $9.6B), KO operating income ($13.8B FY2025, $10.0B FY2024), and PEP operating income ($11.5B FY2025, $12.9B FY2024) all match the underlying data within normal rounding. The characterization of KO's FY2025 earnings rebound and PEP's margin compression is directionally accurate and well-supported. One minor note: PEP's FY2024 net income was $9.578B, which the analyst rounds to $9.6B — acceptable rounding. The citations reference KO concepts only; PEP data is implicitly grounded but not explicitly cited by citation tag, which is a minor citation hygiene issue rather than a factual error.
missed_evidence: KO FY2023 and FY2024 intermediate revenue figures ($45.754B and $47.061B) are not mentioned, which would show the revenue growth was largely front-loaded in FY2022–FY2023 and slowed materially in FY2024–FY2025.

PEP FY2023 gross profit ($49.590B) and FY2024 gross profit ($50.110B) are omitted; including them would show PEP gross profit growth also slowed significantly, reinforcing the margin compression narrative.
KO FY2022 operating income ($10.909B) is not cited, which would contextualize the FY2024 dip to $9.992B as a meaningful step-down before the FY2025 rebound to $13.762B.
PEP FY2022 net income ($8.910B) is not mentioned; noting that PEP's FY2025 net income ($8.240B) is actually below its FY2022 level would strengthen the margin compression narrative.
No explicit citation tags for PEP quantitative data are provided (all citations reference KO concepts), creating an asymmetric citation trail.

cross_axis: The profitability axis does not contextualize KO's unusually large FY2025 net income jump (from $10.631B to $13.107B, +23%) relative to its operating income jump (from $9.992B to $13.762B, +38%), which may reflect below-the-line items (e.g., tax benefits, equity income) worth flagging for other axes such as quality-of-earnings or cash flow.

The most striking divergence is at the bottom line in FY2025: KO's net income surged to $13.1B [KO:Q7] (a net margin of ~27.3%) while PEP's net income fell to $8.2B [PEP:Q7] (a net margin of ~8.8%), a sharp reversal from PEP's $9.6B in FY2024 [PEP:Q6]. Operating income tells a similar story—KO's operating income jumped to $13.8B in FY2025 [KO:Q11] after a dip to $10.0B in FY2024 [KO:Q10], whereas PEP's operating income declined to $11.5B in FY2025 [PEP:Q11] from a peak of $12.9B in FY2024 [PEP:Q10]. Overall, KO demonstrates superior margin quality and a strong FY2025 earnings rebound, while PEP faces margin compression despite its larger revenue base.

Overall, KO demonstrates superior margin quality and a strong FY2025 earnings rebound, while PEP faces margin compression despite its larger revenue base.

The narrative flows logically and the dual-company breakdown adds useful granularity. Citation tags are used consistently, and the report proactively flags the limitation around PEP net income citation enumeration rather than silently asserting the figures—this is a strong example of epistemic honesty. The 'Insufficient data' call on Dividend Payout Ratio is appropriate and appropriately brief.
